title: Dhole (Asiatic Wild Dog)
focus: Habitat, characteristics, and conservation status of the Dhole.
rationale: Dhole is a distinct species with specific IUCN (Endangered) and WPA (Schedule I) statuses, making it a prime candidate for UPSC environment questions.
sectionExcerpt: Dhole has made a comeback in Assam's Kaziranga-Karbi Anglong Landscape. It inhabits Eastern & Western Ghats, Assam, Meghalaya, West Bengal, Ladakh, etc. It is a carnivore resembling a small wolf, hunting mainly in the early morning or late evening. Conservation Status: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix II, IUCN Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Comeback in Assam's Kaziranga-Karbi Anglong Landscape (KKAL).
Habitat includes Eastern & Western Ghats, Assam, Meghalaya, West Bengal, Ladakh, and countries like Cambodia, Nepal, Bhutan, Thailand.
Carnivorous, hunts in early morning or late evening.
Conservation: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix II, IUCN Endangered.

title: Alpine Musk Deer
focus: Habitat, unique physical traits, threats, and conservation status of the Alpine Musk Deer.
rationale: The mix-up with the Himalayan counterpart and its unique physical characteristics (fangs, no antlers) make it highly testable.
sectionExcerpt: Central Zoo Authority reported a mix-up in identifying Alpine musk deer for conservation breeding. Native to India, Nepal, Bhutan, and China, it inhabits alpine meadows and fir forests. It is solitary, shy, and herbivorous, uniquely possessing fangs but lacking antlers and a gall bladder. Threatened by poaching for muskpods. Conservation Status: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Native to India, Nepal, Bhutan, and China; inhabits alpine meadows and fir forests.
Unique physical traits: possesses fangs, lacks antlers and gall bladder.
Threatened by poaching for muskpods used in perfumes and traditional medicines.
Conservation: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.

title: Indian Grey Wolf
focus: Habitat, characteristics, and conservation status of the Indian Grey Wolf.
rationale: Its specific niche in arid/semi-arid plains and recent threats from dog attacks in Maharashtra are relevant for ecology questions.
sectionExcerpt: The Indian grey wolf faces risks from dog attacks in Kadbanwadi grassland, Maharashtra. It inhabits Peninsular India, including Rajasthan and Gujarat. A subspecies of the grey wolf, it travels in smaller packs, is less vocal, and occupies a top predator niche in arid and semi-arid open plains and grasslands. Conservation Status: WPA Schedule I.
evidenceBullets: Faces risk from dog attacks in Kadbanwadi grassland, Maharashtra.
Habitat: Peninsular India including Rajasthan, Gujarat, etc.
Subspecies of grey wolf; travels in smaller packs and is less vocal.
Top predator in arid and semi-arid open plains and grasslands of India.

title: Bonobo
focus: Habitat, behavioral characteristics, and conservation status of the Bonobo.
rationale: The discovery of compositionality in their vocal communication and their genetic proximity to humans make them a unique topic for science and environment intersections.
sectionExcerpt: A study found that vocal communication between Bonobos shows compositionality, similar to human language. Found only in the forests of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). Like Chimpanzees, they share 98.7% of DNA with humans. They are peaceful, female-led, and smaller/darker than chimpanzees. Conservation Status: CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Vocal communication shows compositionality, similar to human language.
Endemic to the forests of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC).
Share 98.7% of DNA with humans, making them our closest living relatives alongside chimpanzees.
Conservation: CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.
